content.full_text:
A-82-053

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance
(916) 322-5662

Administration
322-5660

Executive/Legal
322-5901

Enforcement
322-6441

Statements of Economic Interest
322-6444

April 15, 1982

Arnold Sterne Leff, M.D.
Director
Health Services Department
2500 Alhambra Avenue
Martinez, CA 94553

Robert H. Kaplan
Executive Director
Contra Costa Health Plan
2500 Alhambra Avenue
Martinez, CA 94553

Re: Advice Letter No. 82-053

Gentlemen:

Thank you for your letter requesting advice from this
office as to your duties under the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.1/ The following advice is
provided to you pursuant to Section 83114(b).

My understanding of the situation is as follows. Both of
you are employees of the Contra Costa County Health Services
Department. In his capacity as Executive Director of the Contra
Costa Health Plan, Mr. Kaplan recommended to the County on
September 8, 1981, that a contract for consulting services with
Henry Leavitt be approved. Mr. Leavitt is Vice President of
Health Sector Management, Inc., a private consulting firm which
specializes in studies on and analyses for prepaid health management systems. The contract was approved and performed.

Subsequently Health Sector Management contacted the two of
you regarding the possibility of your participating with them
in a consulting contract for the City and County of San Francisco.
The contract calls for a feasibility study of organized health
system options for San Francisco General Hospital. Both of you
have since participated in some consultation sessions with
Health Sector Management, Inc., for this contract with San Francisco.
You anticipate receiving approximately $5,000 each in total
compensation for this contract.

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Arnold Sterne Leff, M.D.
Robert H. Kaplan
April 15, 1982
Page Two


     You asked whether any conflicts of interest could arise
out of this situation.

     First, I would like to point out that this office renders
advice only on the Political Reform Act.  There may be other
laws which are relevant to this matter.  The County Counsel or
Attorney General's Office can assist you with the interpretation
of other conflict of interest laws.

     The Act does not prohibit these consulting activities;
however, it does prohibit you as public officials from participating
in any governmental decision in which you have a financial
interest.  Section 87103.  An official has a financial interest
in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on any business entity or real
property in which the official has an investment of $1,000 or
more, or on any source of income of $250 or more to the official
within the previous 12 months.  Section 87103.

     So long as Health Sector Management, Inc., is a source of
income of over $250 to you, you will have to disqualify yourselves
from all decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial
effect on Health Sector Management, Inc.2/  These decisions
would clearly include decisions as to whether Health Sector
Management, Inc. will receive contracts for consulting services
from the County.

     I trust that this discussion has been helpful.  If I can
be further assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916)
322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division
DMP:plh
Enclosure

_________________________________
  2/  Commission Regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702
(copy enclosed), further defines what constitutes a "material
financial effect" on a business entity.

870-3

Health Services Department
OFFICE OF THE DIRECTOR

Hospital & Administration
2500 Alhambra Avenue
Martinez, California 94553
(415) 372-4200

March 19, 1982

Barbara Milman, Division Chief
Legal Division of the Fair
Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Building
P.O. Box 187
Sacramento, Calif. 95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

We are writing you to request an informal advice letter from the California
Fair Political Practices Commission on some proposed consulting work that
we are doing on our own time. We both are employees of the Contra Costa
County Health Services Department and were asked by Health Sector Management, Inc.
to participate with them in consulting work for the City and County of
San Francisco. Our reason for an opinion is because of the potential for,
or appearance of, a conflict of interest between Health Sector Management, Inc.
and our county employment. We are enclosing for your review all the pertinent
documentation that may assist you in writing an opinion.

Below, we briefly outline chronologically, the events that have occurred:

1) September 8, 1981, a memorandum from Robert Kaplan signed by
Arnold Steme Leff, M.D. to Mel Wingett, County Administrator
asking for approval of a contract with Mr. Henry Leavitt for
the term 9/15/81 to 3/15/82. (Attachment A)

At the time we entered into a contract with Henry Leavitt for
his services for Contra Costa County, we did not have any
knowledge of the City and County of San Francisco's interest
in a study on an organized health system.

2) On September 16, 1981, the City and County of San Francisco
released an RFP proposal outlining the details of the desired
study. (Attachment B) On September 21, 1981, the City and
County of San Francisco issued an addendum to their RFP.
(Attachment B-1)

Contra Costa County

3) On October 5, 1981, Mr. Henry Leavitt, Vice-President of Health
Sector Management, Inc. contacted us to see if we would participate
with them in responding to the proposal for San Francisco.

4) On October 13, 1981, Health Sector Management, Inc. submitted a
proposal to conduct the feasibility study. We agreed to help them
out in this study. We did this without a formal contractual
arrangement with Health Sector Management, Inc. (Attachment C)

5) December 1, 1981, we found out that Health Sector Management, Inc.
was 1 of 4 finalists with the San Francisco contract. At that time,
we sent the attached memorandum to Mel Wingett, County Administrator,
alerting him of the situation. (Attachment D)

6) On December 9, 1981, the City and County of San Francisco selected
Health Sector Management, Inc. to conduct the feasibility study
for a county-based organized health system.

7) On December 16, 1981, we requested an opinion of a private attorney -
Mr. Keith Howard, of Tining & DeLap. (Attachment E)

8) On December 28, 1981, Mr. Howard reviewed the potential relationship
with Health Sector Management, Inc. and the Conflict of Interest Code
of the Health Services Department of Contra Costa County. He found
nothing in the Conflict of Interest Code prohibiting the relationship
by us and Health Sector Management, Inc. (Attachment F)

9) On January 20, 1982, a memorandum was sent to Mel Wingett, County
Administrator asking for a formal opinion of County Counsel on conflict
of interest. (Attachment G)

10) March 4, 1982 memorandum from John Clauson to Mel Wingett regarding
proposed consulting work between Robert Kaplan, Arnold Sterne Leff and
Health Sector Management, Inc. The opinion of the County Counsel
suggested we write the Fair Political Practices Commission for an
opinion. (Attachment H)

11) March 17, 1982, Mr. Kaplan spoke with an attorney in the legal division
of the California Fair Political Practices Commission and suggested we
compile all of the information enclosed and request an informal advice
letter.


At the time the original contact dated September 8, 1981 with the Health
Sector Management, Inc. was proposed by the County, we had absolutely no
knowledge of the San Francisco upcoming project. We agreed to participate with
Health Sector Management, Inc. without giving thought to their selection. As
soon as we learned of them as finalists, we acted to find out whether this would

be a conflict of interest or not. We have participated in some consultation
sessions with Health Sector Management, Inc. for the City and County of
San Francisco already. We have done this on our own time, and have not and
will not accept any payment for these services until we have a written
opinion from you on this matter.

If there is any additional information or questions which you may have,
please feel free to contact either of us.

Sincerely,

Robert H. Kaplan, Executive Director
Contra Costa Health Plan

Arnold Sterne Leff, M.D., Director
Health Services Department

RK:ASL:cbc
Attachments

sections.question: Whether any conflicts of interest could arise from the situation where two county health services employees are participating as consultants with Health Sector Management, Inc. on a San Francisco contract, given that they previously recommended approval of a contract with the same firm for the County.
sections.conclusion: The Act does not prohibit these consulting activities; however, it does prohibit you as public officials from participating in any governmental decision in which you have a financial interest. Section 87100. So long as Health Sector Management, Inc., is a source of income of over $250 to you, you will have to disqualify yourselves from all decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Health Sector Management, Inc. These decisions would clearly include decisions as to whether Health Sector Management, Inc., will receive contracts for consulting services from the County.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the requesters, as county employees, would face conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act by participating in consulting work with Health Sector Management, Inc., a firm they previously recommended for a county contract.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that while the Political Reform Act does not prohibit the consulting activities, the requesters must disqualify themselves from any governmental decisions that would have a material financial effect on Health Sector Management, Inc., specifically including decisions regarding whether that firm receives county contracts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated March 9, 1982) and the FPPC response letter (dated April 15, 1982). Both sections were analyzed. The advice letter is clearly labeled with standard FPPC response format from Counsel Diane Marohisburn. Statutory references to Government Code Sections 81000, 83114(b), 87100, and 87103 are cited.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether any conflicts of interest could arise from the situation where two county health services employees are participating as consultants with Health Sector Management, Inc. on a San Francisco contract, given that they previously recommended approval of a contract with the same firm for the County.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prohibit these consulting activities; however, it does prohibit you as public officials from participating in any governmental decision in which you have a financial interest. Section 87100. So long as Health Sector Management, Inc., is a source of income of over $250 to you, you will have to disqualify yourselves from all decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Health Sector Management, Inc. These decisions would clearly include decisions as to whether Health Sector Management, Inc., will receive contracts for consulting services from the County.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether two Contra Costa County health services employees have conflicts of interest by participating as consultants with Health Sector Management, Inc. on a San Francisco project, when they had previously recommended the firm for county work. The FPPC concludes the activities are not prohibited but require disqualification from certain county decisions.
